behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-queue handoff: signature validation must gate enqueueing. A pure Unit test of HMAC comparison misses the real failure mode ('body is not forwarded'). No real OS/network/subprocess is required; in-process wiring of handler + verifier + queue spy/fake reproduces it. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require real HTTP/network timing, only correct reject-and-not-forward semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Run the HTTP handler in-process with real signature-check code and a fake/spy queue module. Send a request with mismatched body/signature, assert status 401, and assert queue write was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across modules (handler response path vs queue persistence call), not single-function logic. Minimum setup is handler + real queue adapter (or durable local store) in-process to observe write-complete before response send. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require real external network/subprocess; ordering can be proven without full System infra.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler to the real queue persistence module backed by local test storage. Instrument/observe write completion and response send, then assert write completes first and only then 200 is returned.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at module interaction between request path and async downstream worker trigger. No real OS/network is required to catch waiting-vs-not-waiting bugs; a delayed downstream stub in-process is sufficient. Rejected higher level: behavior text is about not awaiting downstream completion, not real infrastructure effects.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process handler with downstream processor stub that blocks (e.g., unresolved promise for several seconds). Assert HTTP response is returned promptly while processor remains unfinished.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This explicitly depends on wall-clock latency under concurrency, which is sensitive to real runtime scheduling/event-loop contention and request handling behavior. In-process mocks/fakes can hide true timing failures. REAL-INFRA is satisfied by explicit wall-clock SLA under concurrent load.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real service process, generate concurrent requests while earlier events trigger intentionally slow downstream work, and measure end-to-end HTTP latency. Assert each new webhook response is <=3s wall-clock.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness depends on state handoff across requests and queue-write boundary, beyond a single pure function. Minimum is multi-module in-process test with persistent/idempotency state and queue write observation. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require real network/subprocess, only idempotent write behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Send two requests with the same event ID through the wired handler and persistent dedup state. Assert first request enqueues once and second request does not create an additional queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This is translation of a queue-layer backpressure signal into HTTP behavior at module boundary. A mock that never emits 'full' would miss it, but an in-process fake/real queue that returns full is enough; no System-only dependency is stated. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require real OS/network timing, only correct mapping to 503 + header.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke handler with queue module configured to return a 'full/capacity exceeded' error. Assert response status is 503 and Retry-After header is present with expected value/format.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets queue component behavior with real local persistence semantics; it is beyond a pure unit but does not inherently need external network/subprocess. Per level defs, local DB/filesystem-backed module wiring fits Integration. Rejected higher level: behavior text asks for capacity enforcement in the queue itself, not OS-level process orchestration.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue with a small configured capacity in test storage, enqueue until full, then attempt one more event and assert explicit rejection/error and no extra persisted item.
